summary: Gruber reflects on a SXSW session he did with Merlin Mann about pursuing creative obsessions on the web. He argues that the best web publishing comes from genuine passion rather than chasing page views and ad revenue. Using Walt Disney's philosophy of making money to fund more creative work, he contrasts integrity-driven publishing with the cynical pageview-farming of 'pro blogging.' He cites Jonathan Coulton's success as a self-published musician as proof that building an audience through honest work eventually generates income. The core formula: obsession times voice — deep knowledge of a topic combined with a distinctive way of expressing it — is what produces great work.
key_points: CPM-driven publishing incentivizes sensationalism, page-splitting, and clickbait over genuine quality
The best nonfiction (and fiction) comes from people who can't stop thinking about a specific topic and develop a distinctive voice for expressing it
You can build a sustainable creative career by obsessing over quality and letting monetization follow naturally
The 'pro blogging' industry wrongly treats blogging as a skill unto itself — the real craft is writing, which is ancient
Walt Disney's philosophy — making money to fund more creative work, not the reverse — is the right orientation for web publishing
money_quotes: We don't make movies to make money; we make money to make more movies.

No one gets into something like this without an obsession, but if your obsession is with the money, and your revenue is directly correlated to page views, then rather than write or produce anything with any actual merit or integrity, you'll dance like a monkey.
It's thievery — not of money, but of readers' attention.

You can obsess over your work, build an audience based on deep mutual respect, and eventually opportunities to earn money from it will present themselves. I don't know how it works, I only know that it does.

The entire quote-unquote 'pro blogging' industry — which exists as the sort of pimply teenage brother to the shirt-and-tie SEO industry — is predicated on the notion that blogging is a meaningful verb. It is not. The verb is writing.
The format and medium are new, but the craft is ancient.
Obsession times voice is a pretty good stab at a simple formula for doing it right.
key_insight: Great web publishing comes from the formula of obsession times voice — deep passion for a subject combined with a distinctive way of expressing it — not from chasing pageviews and ad revenue.
